source: iConnections Global Alts 2025 — The Future of AI
summary: Foundation model companies without proprietary data will become commodities, validated by DeepSeek R1
summary_extended: Baker argues that foundation model companies lacking proprietary data moats are on a path to commoditization, a thesis he claims was validated by the release of DeepSeek R1, which open-sourced the replication of OpenAI's reasoning model methodology and made it universally accessible. His core reasoning is that once the training approach is freely available, the only durable competitive advantage is exclusive access to unique, high-quality data — something most pure-play model companies do not possess. He specifically identifies OpenAI as the most vulnerable, lacking a proprietary data asset comparable to Meta's Facebook/Instagram, Google's YouTube, or xAI's X/Twitter corpus. The investment implication is that companies with large, unique, and defensible data assets embedded in their foundation model efforts (META, GOOGL, and potentially xAI if it were public) are structurally advantaged, while OpenAI and similar API-first model providers face existential margin compression. Baker frames this not as a new prediction but as a prior call now confirmed by DeepSeek's release, suggesting high conviction in the near-term acceleration of this dynamic.
detail: DeepSeek R1 open-sourced the replication of OpenAI's reasoning model approach, making the methodology universally accessible. Companies without unique data moats (like xAI with X/Twitter, Meta with Facebook/Instagram, Google with YouTube) face existential commoditization pressure. OpenAI specifically called out as vulnerable.
confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as a thesis validated by DeepSeek, references it as a prediction made at last year's panel now proven correct, and speaks with no hedging language.
quote_fragment: any model that didn't have proprietary data was basically going to become a commodity

ticker_audit.reasoning: Baker explicitly names Meta's Facebook and Instagram as proprietary data moats that insulate it from the commoditization pressure facing pure-play model companies, making META a direct beneficiary of this thesis.

Baker explicitly names Google's YouTube as a proprietary data moat that gives it structural advantage over foundation model companies without unique data, making GOOGL a direct beneficiary of this thesis.

MSFT is deeply tied to OpenAI via investment and Azure, but Baker's thesis frames OpenAI as the vulnerable party lacking a data moat — MSFT is not cited as having a proprietary data advantage in this context and is not a beneficiary of the commoditization dynamic described. Its inclusion appears to be bleed-over from adjacent discussion.
